dependabot_config.raw_snippet:
# To get started with Dependabot version updates, you'll need to specify which
# package ecosystems to update and where the package manifests are located.
# Please see the documentation for all configuration options:
# https://help.github.com/github/administering-a-repository/configuration-options-for-dependency-updates

version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "gradle" # See documentation for possible values
    directory: "/" # Location of package manifests
    schedule:
      interval: "dai

prs.title: Fix issue 230 - Router uses inactive layers
Fix endless passes if impossible to route nets were found
DSN Loader Isolation — Refactor to a Self-Contained, Framework-Independent I/O Layer
feat: allow GET /jobs/{jobId}/output to return partial output for in-progress jobs
API authentication toggle and authentication providers
Enhanced logging and debug control and documentation
Add version v1.9 source code, compilation and comparison scripts
Add API Server HTTP CORS Header Support
Reduce the complexity of the codebase by removing not-used features
Remove fanout feature and related code
KiCad compatible Design Rules Checker (DRC) with CLI support
Progressed & Bugs Fixed & Remove old freerouting-laucher
Add modular API key validation with Google Sheets provider
Add DRC infrastructure with KiCad-compatible JSON output
Fix copper to edge clearance not respected on non-zero layers
Fix potential memory leaks and reduce memory consumption
Feat: add support for user-supplied random seed (fixes #545)
Correctly update max value in Clearance-Matrix
Avoid modifying collection while iterating over it (related to issue #491)
Update Japanese translation and Fix some translation issues
Remove Unicode character PHI (U+03A6) in Kicad-plugin
Create a docker build and push workflow for ghcr (Github Container Registry)
fix: export for dto added, required when only API is run

entries.title: Enhanced logging and debug control and documentation
Add support for intelligent copper plane/pour routing in autorouter

channels.name: docker-local-build
channels.note: No prebuilt image check attempted; user builds locally

per_channel.note: Docker local-build path; no registry digest checked

gitleaks.note: gitleaks not installed; install to cover secrets-in-history

coverage_affirmations.gitleaks_explanation: gitleaks not installed; install to cover secrets-in-history

solo_maintainer.verbatim_sentence: Note: andrasfuchs accounts for 87% of all commits. This is a solo-maintainer project — all governance findings should be read in that context.

entries.title: Java ObjectInputStream.readObject() on user-loaded board files — textbook deserialization RCE class
entries.action_hint: Do NOT open `.binsnapshot` / serialized board files from untrusted sources until freerouting migrates away from Java native serialization.
entries.threat_model_context: freerouting's `BasicBoard.java` at line 108-110 constructs a `new ObjectInputStream(input_stream)` and calls `readObject()` on it — the exact API pattern that Java deserialization RCE exploits (ysoserial-class gadget chains) target. The class implements `Serializable` and has a custom `readObject(ObjectInputStream)` hook (line 1551). 34 additional Java source files import `ObjectInputStream`, spanning board/gui/interactive/rules packages — Java serialization is core to freerouting's data-persistence model. Board state, settings, and window snapshots are all persisted via ObjectInputStream/ObjectOutputStream. freerouting loads PCB designs from user-provided files (DSN and serialized binary formats), which is the documented consumer workflow. A malicious board file crafted to exploit a deserialization gadget chain against freerouting's classpath would trigger arbitrary Java code execution on the user's machine at file-open time.
entries.what_this_means: If a user opens a malicious .binsnapshot (or any serialized board file) obtained from an untrusted source — a shared community project, an email attachment, a file from a compromised sharing site — the file-open action can trigger arbitrary code execution via Java deserialization gadget chains. The specific exploitability depends on which gadget libraries are on freerouting's classpath (commons-collections, Spring, Groovy, log4j, etc.), but the attack class is well-documented and actively weaponized.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a remote-triggerable vulnerability — the user has to voluntarily open a malicious file. Not evidence that a specific CVE or PoC exists for freerouting today. But the structural vulnerability is unambiguous: Java `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` of untrusted data is a known-exploitable RCE class, ranked in the OWASP Top 10 for over a decade.
entries.date_label: Since freerouting's Java serialization model was established (repo created 2014-06-28)
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: migrate board persistence away from Java native serialization toward a safe format (JSON/Protobuf/custom parser). As an interim mitigation, add an `ObjectInputFilter` (Java 9+) that whitelists expected classes. Consumer-side: only open board files from trusted sources until the migration lands; consider running freerouting in a container or VM with the board file pre-staged, isolated from credential-bearing host files.

entries.title: Solo-maintainer concentration (andrasfuchs 86.7%) + anti-destruction-only ruleset + 9.5% formal review
entries.action_hint: Pin to a reviewed release version; avoid auto-updating; treat new releases as attention-worthy.
entries.threat_model_context: andrasfuchs holds 86.7% of top-6 contributor commits — solo-maintainer flag fires (E3). The repo has 1 ruleset with 2 rules (`deletion` + `non_fast_forward`) but these are anti-destruction rules that prevent force-push and branch deletion; they do NOT require review before merge (E4). No CODEOWNERS. Formal-review rate is 9.5% across the 105-PR sample (E5). A compromised andrasfuchs account would reach the next release with no structural review gate — the anti-destruction rules would prevent rewriting history but not prevent a malicious commit from landing on master and being released.
entries.what_this_means: The governance gate that could catch a compromised-account scenario exists minimally (anti-destruction rules) but does not include a review-requirement step. Consumer mitigation is pinning to a specific reviewed release rather than tracking latest.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of malicious behavior. andrasfuchs has been the primary maintainer for 12 years; this is a normal small-project governance pattern. The finding describes the structural exposure.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: extend the ruleset to include a `pull_request` rule requiring ≥1 approving review before merge; add CODEOWNERS naming a backup reviewer. Consumer-side: pin freerouting to a specific version tag; read release notes before upgrading.

entries.title: No SECURITY.md + 0 published advisories in 12 years despite confirmed RCE-class surface (F0)
entries.action_hint: Use GitHub's `Report a vulnerability` button if you find a freerouting-specific exploit.
entries.threat_model_context: community/profile has_security_policy=false. security-advisories count: 0 across 12 years of development. Given F0's confirmed Java-deserialization surface on user-loaded files, the zero-advisory record is especially notable — either (a) no exploit has been reported by a researcher, (b) issues were silently fixed without GHSA publication, or (c) awareness of the attack class has not reached the project. Consistent with the catalog-wide silent-fix pattern but more consequential here given F0's confirmed severity.
entries.what_this_means: No documented disclosure channel; no GHSA syndication. A researcher with a freerouting-specific deserialization gadget chain has no documented path to disclose privately.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of suppressed vulns. Consistent with a small-project disclosure-process gap.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: add a 1-page SECURITY.md naming GitHub's `Report a vulnerability` UI + expected response window. Given F0, this is especially high-value.

entries.title: 373-day release stall — v2.1.0 from 2025-04-12 is the latest tag despite active main
entries.action_hint: Users on the latest release are ~1 year behind main; main-branch fixes (if any) do not reach installed users.
entries.threat_model_context: Latest release v2.1.0: 2025-04-12 (373 days before scan). Prior cadence was 3-4 months (v2.0.1 Nov 2024, v2.0.0 Nov 2024, v1.9.0 Oct 2023). Main is active — last push 2026-04-19, one day before scan. Any F0-class mitigations merged to main since April 2025 are not reaching users installed from release tags. Consistent pattern with wezterm (entry 21, 807-day stall) — active-main-without-release-tagging is a recurring governance shape.
entries.what_this_means: Consumers on v2.1.0 are running April-2025 code. If a fix for F0 (Java deserialization mitigation) lands on main tomorrow, users don't receive it until a new release tag is cut and the JAR/Docker/MSI pipelines rebuild.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not evidence of abandonment — main is active. The gap is tag-to-consumer latency, not project liveness.
entries.how_to_fix: Maintainer-side: cut a new release (even v2.1.1) to push accumulated main-branch fixes to consumers. The release automation is already configured (docker-release workflow + MSI build) — it's a human-process decision to invoke it.

entries.title: Dependabot on Gradle + active dep-update cadence — all 5 open PRs are dep bumps
entries.action_hint: Positive operational signal — context for reading F0-F3 severity.
entries.threat_model_context: dependabot.yml is present with `- package-ecosystem: gradle` — the Java dep graph is watched for CVE-driven updates. All 5 currently-open PRs are Dependabot bumps (log4j-core, google-cloud-core, gax, google-auth-library, rewrite-static-analysis). This is a real operational investment — compare to wezterm (entry 21) + kanata (entry 23) where Cargo ecosystem is unwatched; here Gradle is actively updated.
entries.what_this_means: Java transitive-dep CVEs surface to the maintainer automatically. Doesn't reduce F0 severity (which is about freerouting's own use of ObjectInputStream, not a transitive dep bug), but does remove one class of supply-chain risk.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Doesn't fix F0's Java-deserialization class. That requires maintainer-side code changes, not dep bumps.

entries.title: Gemini AI workflows handle issue triage + PR review — distinctive governance infrastructure
entries.action_hint: Non-blocking context for how the repo operates.
entries.threat_model_context: 5 Gemini-AI workflows are active: Dispatch, Invoke, Review, Scheduled Triage, Triage. freerouting has delegated issue-triage + PR-comment review to Google Gemini. Different AI-governance pattern from QuickLook's Copilot SWE agent (entry 22, which authors PRs) — here the AI reviews human-authored PRs. 32.4% any-review rate (E5) partly reflects Gemini's PR comments; 9.5% formal APPROVED rate reflects human-reviewer endorsements specifically.
entries.what_this_means: The governance infrastructure includes automated review; a PR opened without human review still typically gets Gemini's analysis. This is informational context — useful for understanding the 32.4% any-review / 9.5% formal split.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a claim about Gemini-review quality. The finding just names the tooling pattern.

entries.title: Scanner gaps on Java/Gradle ecosystem + Java-specific distribution channels
entries.action_hint: Maven/Gradle parsing + Docker-registry channel detection would close this family of gaps.
entries.threat_model_context: Two gaps specific to this scan: (1) Gradle dependency parsing not implemented — runtime_count=0 despite build.gradle + active Dependabot alerts showing real dep surface (E12); (2) Distribution-channel detection surfaced only fictitious 'docker-local-build' when real channels are JAR on GitHub Releases, Docker images on ghcr.io, and MSI installer (E11). Same language-ecosystem-gap pattern as Go (Xray-core), Ruby (kamal), Rust (wezterm/kanata), C# (QuickLook).
entries.what_this_means: For this scan, Java dep-CVE surface is not assessed by the harness. Mitigation: Dependabot IS catching updates upstream (F4), so the practical dep-CVE coverage is actually healthy — just not via our scanner.
entries.what_this_does_not_mean: Not a freerouting defect. Property of the scanner.
entries.how_to_fix: Scanner-side: add Maven/Gradle parsing with GitHub Security Advisory / OSV.dev Maven queries; add Docker-registry + MSI channel detection for Java tools.

entries.claim: freerouting calls `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` on user-provided input streams — the canonical Java deserialization RCE vector. Confirmed at src_v19/main/java/app/freerouting/board/BasicBoard.java:108-110.
entries.result: Line 108-110: `ObjectInputStream object_stream = new ObjectInputStream(input_stream); return (BasicBoard) object_stream.readObject();`. Also line 1551: `private void readObject(ObjectInputStream p_stream) throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException` — the class implements a custom readObject hook. BasicBoard `implements Serializable` (line 43). This is the textbook Java-deserialization RCE class — a maliciously-crafted `.binsnapshot` or serialized board file can trigger a gadget-chain attack on the user's machine when the file is opened.

entries.claim: The `ObjectInputStream` import appears in 35 source files across board/, gui/, interactive/, rules/ packages. Java serialization is core to freerouting's data-persistence model (saving/loading board state, settings, snapshots).
entries.result: 35 files match the deserialization regex — all via `import java.io.ObjectInputStream`. Coverage includes: BasicBoard.java, Communication.java, BoardRules.java, Settings.java, BoardHandling.java, BoardHandlingHeadless.java, WindowSnapshot.java, BoardFrame.java, WindowRouteParameter.java, WindowObjectListWithFilter.java, and 25 more. The pattern is consistent — serializable state objects with readObject/writeObject pairs for persistence.

entries.claim: Solo-maintainer concentration — andrasfuchs holds 1,544 of 1,741 top-6 contributor commits (86.7%, above the 80% solo-maintainer threshold). Second contributor miho has 116 commits (6.7%).
entries.result: andrasfuchs: 1544; miho: 116; dependabot[bot]: 41; maksz42: 33; rusefillc: 28; L1uTongweiNewAccount: 19. total_contributor_count: 36. Top-1 share 86.7% — solo-maintainer flag fires.

entries.claim: Branch-protection posture is partial — 1 ruleset with 2 rules (`deletion` + `non_fast_forward`) on the default branch. These are anti-destruction rules (prevent force-push / branch deletion), NOT review-requirement rules. No CODEOWNERS.
entries.result: classic: HTTP 404. rulesets: {count: 1}. rules_on_default: 2 entries — type='deletion' (blocks branch deletion), type='non_fast_forward' (blocks force-push). No pull_request rule. No approving_review_count requirement. The ruleset protects the branch from destruction but does NOT enforce review before merge. CODEOWNERS: absent.

entries.claim: PR review posture: 9.5% formal-review rate, 32.4% any-review rate, 19 self-merges across a 105-PR sample. Combined with the anti-destruction-only ruleset (E4), the effective review gate is minimal.
entries.result: sample_size: 105; formal_review_rate: 9.5; any_review_rate: 32.4; self_merge_count: 19 (18%); total_merged_lifetime: (matches sample_size). The 32.4% any-review rate reflects Gemini-AI-assisted triage workflows commenting on PRs (see E9) plus human comments; 9.5% formal APPROVED decisions is below typical project gates.

entries.claim: community/profile reports has_security_policy=false despite 12 years of development on a file-parsing EDA tool. Positives: has_contributing=true, has_code_of_conduct=true, health_percentage=75%. security-advisories count: 0.
entries.result: health_percentage: 75 (above most V1.2 catalog entries). has_contributing: true. has_code_of_conduct: true. has_security_policy: false. security-advisories count: 0. For a tool with confirmed Java-deserialization RCE surface (F0) and 12 years of history, zero advisories is a notable disclosure-transparency gap.

entries.claim: Release cadence has slowed — latest release v2.1.0 on 2025-04-12, one year before scan. Prior cadence was 3-4 months between minor releases. v2.0.0 was 2024-11-06; v1.9.0 was 2023-10-30.
entries.result: total_count: 15. Recent releases: v2.1.0 (2025-04-12), v2.0.1 (2024-11-14), v2.0.0 (2024-11-06), v1.9.0 (2023-10-30). Gap from v2.1.0 to scan = 373 days. Main is pushed on 2026-04-19 (1 day before scan) — active development, no release landing. Fix-to-user latency for any F0-class finding resolution is substantial.

entries.claim: Dependabot is configured for Gradle (Java deps). All 5 currently-open PRs are Dependabot dependency bumps — the dep-watch layer is functioning.

entries.result: dependabot_config.present: true; ecosystems_tracked: ['gradle']. Open PRs (all 5, by app/dependabot): #600 google-cloud-core 2.62.1→2.62.2, #599 gax 2.72.1→2.72.2, #598 log4j-core 2.25.2→2.25.3, #597 google-auth-library 1.40.0→1.41.0, #596 rewrite-static-analysis 2.23.0→2.25.0. Positive operational signal — dep updates flow automatically.

entries.claim: 17 GitHub Actions workflows include 5 Gemini-AI workflows (Dispatch, Invoke, Review, Scheduled Triage, Triage) — freerouting uses Google Gemini AI for automated issue triage + PR review. Distinctive governance pattern.
entries.result: 17 workflows total. Gemini-AI suite: gemini-dispatch.yml, gemini-invoke.yml, gemini-review.yml, gemini-scheduled-triage.yml, gemini-triage.yml. Also: release, snapshot, pages, docker-nightly, docker-release, and build pipelines. pull_request_target_count: 0. The Gemini-AI integration is a different governance pattern from QuickLook's Copilot-SWE-agent (entry 22) — there the AI writes PRs; here the AI triages/reviews.

section_01.pill_row: 1.7k⭐ · GPL-3.0 · Java · 1 critical · 3 warnings · 3 info · CRITICAL
section_01.summary: freerouting is a 12-year-old Java PCB auto-router by andrasfuchs — a specialist EDA tool used in KiCad workflows. Critical verdict driven by F0: `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` is invoked on user-loaded input streams at BasicBoard.java:108-110, with 35 files across the codebase using the same Java-serialization pattern. Opening a maliciously-crafted serialized board file triggers the textbook Java-deserialization RCE class on the user's machine. Solo-maintainer concentration (86.7%), an anti-destruction-only ruleset (no review-requirement), and a 373-day release stall compound the risk. Positive signals exist: community health 75% (CONTRIBUTING + CoC present), Dependabot active on Gradle, Gemini-AI triage workflows.

section_02.pill_row: F0 Critical · F1 Warning · F2 Warning · F3 Warning · F4 Info (positive) · F5 Info · F6 Info

section_06.summary: One scorecard override on Q4: Phase 3 advisory computed amber via the standard rubric (q4_has_critical_on_default_path=False by driver convention — the signal is expected to be set by Phase 4 authoring). Phase 4 authored F0 Critical (Java ObjectInputStream RCE class on the documented consumer file-loading path), which makes the default-path-critical condition true in judgment. override_reason = signal_vocabulary_gap — the compute signal vocabulary doesn't directly read from dangerous_primitives.deserialization.hit_count; a widening that auto-fires q4_has_critical_on_default_path when deserialization hits >= N on a file-loading tool would close the override loop. Q1 red genuine (solo-maintainer 86.7% + 9.5% formal review), Q2 green (active Dependabot + main), Q3 amber (CONTRIBUTING + CoC present, SECURITY.md absent).

split_axis_decision.reasoning: Single consumer profile. The JAR / MSI / Docker install channels all install the same freerouting application with the same F0 exposure when opening user-provided board files.

groups.headline: Critical — F0 is the blocking item
groups.one_line_summary: Java ObjectInputStream.readObject() on user-loaded board files — confirmed RCE-class surface.

items.claim: BasicBoard.java:108-110 invokes readObject() on user-provided input streams; 35 files import ObjectInputStream; serialization is core to data-persistence model.

groups.headline: Warning — F1 + F2 + F3 describe governance + release posture
groups.one_line_summary: Solo-maintainer + anti-destruction-only ruleset + no SECURITY.md + 373-day release stall.

items.claim: andrasfuchs 86.7% share; ruleset has `deletion` + `non_fast_forward` rules but no review-requirement; 9.5% formal review; no CODEOWNERS.
No SECURITY.md + 0 published advisories in 12 years despite F0 RCE-class surface on a user-file-loading tool.
v2.1.0 (2025-04-12) is 373 days old; main is active but no new release tagged; fix-to-user latency for any F0 mitigation is substantial.

groups.headline: Info — F4 + F5 + F6
groups.one_line_summary: Dependabot active (positive); Gemini-AI triage; scanner ecosystem gaps.

items.claim: **Positive**: Dependabot on Gradle — 5 open dep-bump PRs showing active update flow. Compare to wezterm/kanata where Cargo is unwatched.
5 Gemini-AI workflows handle PR review + issue triage. Distinctive governance pattern; partly explains 32.4% any-review / 9.5% formal gap.
Scanner ecosystem gaps (Gradle parsing + Java distribution channels — JAR / MSI / Docker).

selections.reason: If freerouting migrates away from ObjectInputStream (e.g., to a JSON persistence layer) or adds an ObjectInputFilter whitelist, F0 collapses to Info and the verdict moves from Critical to Caution.
If the ruleset is extended with a `pull_request` review-requirement rule, F1 softens materially — the anti-destruction-only current state becomes full-review protection.
A new release tag (v2.1.1 or v2.2.0) would close F3's release-stall window; any F0 mitigations merged to main between April 2025 and a new release would reach users on the next install.

entries.headline: Do NOT open `.binsnapshot` / serialized board files from untrusted sources
entries.non_technical: freerouting loads its board state via Java's ObjectInputStream — the textbook Java deserialization RCE vector. A malicious serialized board file can trigger arbitrary code execution on your machine at file-open time. Only open files you generated yourself or received from trusted collaborators.
entries.command: # Nothing to run — this is a file-hygiene rule

entries.headline: Install via GitHub Releases JAR or MSI — pin to v2.1.0 (current)
entries.non_technical: Download freerouting-2.1.0-executable.jar from the v2.1.0 GitHub Release. Verify download integrity via a hash you record yourself (no .sig files are published).

entries.headline: If you must open a suspicious file: isolate in a container or VM
entries.non_technical: Run freerouting inside a Docker container (ghcr.io/freerouting/freerouting) or a disposable VM. Mount only the specific board file + a scratch workspace. If the file triggers deserialization RCE, the attacker gets the container/VM, not your host credentials.

entries.headline: Prefer DSN (Specctra) format imports over serialized snapshots where possible
entries.non_technical: DSN is a text-based format parsed by freerouting's custom parser, not Java ObjectInputStream. It has its own parser-bug risk surface but is NOT the F0 deserialization class. For exchanging boards with others, DSN is the safer interchange format.
entries.command: # Ask sender to export as DSN rather than a binary snapshot

entries.headline: Monitor dependabot alerts on the Java dep graph if building from source
entries.non_technical: Dependabot is active (F4). If building from source, watch the PR queue for security-relevant dep bumps. The maintainer-side dep-watch is functioning; consumer-side just means don't ignore Dependabot-authored PRs in your own forks.

executable_file_inventory.lead: freerouting ships as a Java application distributed via GitHub Releases (executable JAR), Windows MSI installer, and Docker images. The JAR runs on any JDK 11+ system; the Docker image wraps the JAR for headless use.
executable_file_inventory.layer1_summary: Primary surface is the Java-compiled JAR. The 521MB repo includes source, built JARs, and extensive release artifacts. No native binary compilation step — pure JVM.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: new ObjectInputStream(input_stream); object_stream.readObject(); (lines 108-110) — textbook Java deserialization RCE vector
layer2_entries.notes: Core board state class implementing Serializable with custom readObject hook. THIS is F0.
Java serialization is pervasive in freerouting's data-persistence model — settings, snapshots, window state, board rules all use the same pattern.

layer2_entries.dangerous_calls: subprocess.run invocations to launch the freerouting JAR from KiCad
layer2_entries.notes: The KiCad-side Python plugin that spawns freerouting. Separate attack surface from F0; user-authored config doesn't flow through this.

executable_file_inventory.closing_note: Installation paths: download executable JAR from GitHub Releases + `java -jar freerouting-2.1.0-executable.jar`; OR Windows MSI; OR Docker images from ghcr.io/freerouting/freerouting. Cargo/npm-style pinned install NOT available — Java distribution is artifact-based.

Dangerous-primitives regex produced 35 deserialization hits — unusually high count, but unlike Rust serde / JS false-positives, these are ALL real Java ObjectInputStream imports. The regex calibration is correct for Java; the finding is F0.

Q4 signal `q4_has_critical_on_default_path` is hardcoded False in the scan-driver convention pending Phase 4 authoring — doesn't read from dangerous_primitives.deserialization hit count. This drove the Q4 override (scorecard cell rationale documents the signal_vocabulary_gap).

does_anyone_check_the_code.short_answer: No — 86.7% solo-maintainer + anti-destruction-only ruleset (no review rule) + 9.5% formal review

do_they_fix_problems_quickly.short_answer: Yes — 0 open security issues, active Dependabot dep-update flow, main actively committed

do_they_tell_you_about_problems.short_answer: Partly — CONTRIBUTING + CoC present (health 75%) but no SECURITY.md + 0 advisories in 12 years despite F0 RCE-class surface

is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.short_answer: No — Java deserialization RCE class on user-loaded board files (F0) is a critical on the default consumer path
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.rationale: The Phase 3 advisory computed amber via compute.py's Q4 rubric (q4_has_critical_on_default_path=False, since that signal is hardcoded False in the build_form driver pending Phase 4 authoring). Phase 4 has now authored F0 — a Critical severity finding confirming `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` is invoked on user-provided input streams (BasicBoard.java:108-110, E1), with 35 files importing ObjectInputStream (E2) indicating Java serialization is core to the data-persistence model. freerouting's primary documented workflow is users opening PCB design files, including serialized board state — the same paths that flow into the deserialization surface. This makes has_critical_on_default_path=True in the judgment-sense, even though the compute signal input is False by driver convention. The `q4_has_critical_on_default_path` signal conceptually exists but its input pathway doesn't read dangerous_primitives.deserialization.hit_count — it relies on Phase 4 setting the bool. This is a signal-vocabulary calibration issue, not a harness coverage issue (the harness correctly detected 35 deserialization hits).
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.edge_case: The signal vocabulary has `q4_has_critical_on_default_path` as a bool input that the Phase 3 driver convention hardcodes False, expecting Phase 4 to set it. A signal widening that reads dangerous_primitives.deserialization hit_count + presence of file-loading surface (install-path reading user files) would auto-fire this without requiring Phase 4 override.
is_it_safe_out_of_the_box.suggested_threshold_adjustment: Add a compute-side derivation: if dangerous_primitives.deserialization.hit_count >= N (e.g., 5) AND the tool is documented as loading user files (README pattern match for 'open file' / 'import' / 'load'), auto-set q4_has_critical_on_default_path=True. This would close the override loop for the Java/Python/similar-serialization-prone-languages case.

catalog_metadata.short_description: Java PCB auto-router — electronic design automation tool for routing printed circuit board designs. 12-year-old project; KiCad integration; loads Specctra DSN format + serialized board snapshots.
catalog_metadata.target_user: Hardware engineer / hobbyist using KiCad or similar EDA tools who needs automated PCB trace routing. Install: download freerouting-X.Y.Z-executable.jar from GitHub Releases + run `java -jar` OR MSI installer on Windows OR Docker image.
catalog_metadata.shape: Java desktop GUI + CLI + Docker distribution + custom serialization (Java ObjectInputStream + DSN format). NEW shape — first Java V1.2 entry in catalog; first EDA / electronic-design-automation category.
catalog_metadata.prior_scan_note: None — first scan of freerouting. Ninth wild V1.2-schema scan (after markitdown 15, ghostty 16, Kronos 17, kamal 18, Xray-core 19, browser_terminal 20, wezterm 21, QuickLook 22, kanata 23).

phase_4_structured_llm.timeline_intro: freerouting lifecycle — 12-year-old andrasfuchs-led project; v2.0 modernization in 2024; release cadence stalled after v2.1.0.
phase_4_structured_llm.evidence_intro: Command-backed evidence from Phase 1 harness + gh api + direct file fetches of BasicBoard.java and related Java sources.
phase_4_structured_llm.coverage_intro: Phase 1 harness coverage with 7 documented gaps — concentrated on Java/Gradle ecosystem and distribution-channel detection. Notably, the dangerous_primitives deserialization regex worked correctly for Java (no false positives) and surfaced the real F0 finding.

editorial_caption.text: freerouting — 1.7k-star Java PCB auto-router, 12-year andrasfuchs solo project (86.7%). Critical verdict via F0: BasicBoard.java:108-110 calls `ObjectInputStream.readObject()` on user-loaded files — textbook Java deserialization RCE. 35 files import ObjectInputStream. Positives: Dependabot on Gradle, community health 75%, Gemini-AI triage.

body_paragraphs: The specific source lines: `BasicBoard.java:108-110` reads `ObjectInputStream object_stream = new ObjectInputStream(input_stream); return (BasicBoard) object_stream.readObject();`. The class declaration at line 43 is `public class BasicBoard implements Serializable`, and there's a custom `private void readObject(ObjectInputStream p_stream)` hook at line 1551 — standard Java-serialization boilerplate that confirms this is actively-used persistence, not a vestigial import.

Beyond BasicBoard, 34 other source files import `java.io.ObjectInputStream`, spanning board/, gui/, interactive/, and rules/ packages. The pattern across these files is the same — serializable state objects with custom readObject/writeObject pairs for loading saved board state, settings, window snapshots, and routing rules. Java serialization is not peripheral to freerouting; it is the persistence model.

Threat model: freerouting's documented workflow is users opening PCB design files. The consumer obtains a board file (from their own work, a collaborator, a community project, an email attachment), opens it in freerouting, and the application reads the file — including deserialized state via ObjectInputStream. A malicious actor who crafts a serialized board file targeting a gadget chain present on freerouting's classpath (commons-collections, log4j, Spring, or any of freerouting's 100+ transitive Maven deps per the Dependabot surface) can achieve arbitrary Java code execution on the user's machine at file-open time.

This is not a theoretical vulnerability class. Java deserialization RCE has been actively weaponized since 2015 (ysoserial), is on the OWASP Top 10, and has produced hundreds of CVEs across enterprise Java software. freerouting's pattern — Serializable implements + readObject() on user-provided streams — is the textbook exploitable form.

Maintainer-side mitigation paths, in ascending order of effort: (a) add `ObjectInputFilter.Config.setSerialFilter()` to restrict deserialization to expected BasicBoard/subclass types only (single-line Java-9+ fix, dramatically narrows the gadget-chain attack surface); (b) migrate board persistence to a safe format (JSON via Jackson / Gson, or a custom text-based serializer matching the existing DSN approach); (c) deprecate the binary snapshot format entirely in favor of DSN-only. Consumer-side mitigation: isolate freerouting in a Docker container (freerouting publishes an official image) or a disposable VM, especially when opening files from untrusted sources.

andrasfuchs holds 1,544 of the 1,741 top-6 contributor commits — 86.7%. The next contributor, miho, has 116 (6.7%). This is solo-maintainer territory by the 80% threshold definition. The 12-year repo history is effectively one person's project with periodic external PR contributions.

The branch-protection story is partial in an informative way: the repo HAS a ruleset with 2 rules on the default branch, but the rules are `deletion` (prevents branch deletion) and `non_fast_forward` (prevents force-push). These are anti-destruction protections — they stop an attacker from rewriting history — but they do NOT require review before merge. A compromised andrasfuchs account can still land a malicious commit on master; the rules just prevent the attacker from covering their tracks afterward.

Combined with 9.5% formal-review rate and no CODEOWNERS, the merge gate that would catch a malicious commit pre-release is effectively absent. The fix is a 3-line ruleset change to add a `pull_request` rule with `required_approving_review_count: 1`. The governance-infrastructure is already in place; one rule addition would convert it from 'anti-destruction' to 'anti-destruction plus review-required'.

Community-health signals are actually above average for the V1.2 catalog: 75% health (vs 50-62% elsewhere), CONTRIBUTING present, CODE_OF_CONDUCT present. But SECURITY.md is absent and 0 GHSA advisories have been published in 12 years. For a tool with F0's confirmed RCE surface, that's not neutral — it means that if a researcher finds a specific deserialization gadget chain tomorrow, there is no documented private channel for them to use and no mechanism for past fixes (if any exist) to have reached consumers via the advisory feed.

The asymmetry — decent community infrastructure but no security-disclosure infrastructure — is actionable: a 1-page SECURITY.md naming the GitHub private-advisory UI would close this without requiring any code change. Given F0, it should be a priority.

Release timeline from the releases API: v2.1.0 on 2025-04-12, v2.0.1 on 2024-11-14, v2.0.0 on 2024-11-06, v1.9.0 on 2023-10-30. Prior cadence was 3-4 months between releases. The gap between v2.1.0 and the scan (2026-04-20) is 373 days — a clear stall. Main is active (pushed 2026-04-19, day before scan), so development continues; the release-tagging step is what has paused.

The consumer implication matters in conjunction with F0: if a mitigation for the Java deserialization surface lands on main (an ObjectInputFilter, a format migration), users on v2.1.0 don't receive it until a new release is tagged and the JAR/MSI/Docker pipelines rebuild. Fix-to-user latency is the concern — same pattern as wezterm (entry 21, 807-day stall) and QuickLook (entry 22, stale `latest` tag) — but sharper here given F0's confirmed severity.

Dependabot is configured for Gradle and actively producing PRs — all 5 currently-open PRs are Dependabot bumps (google-cloud-core, gax, log4j-core, google-auth-library, rewrite-static-analysis). That's a real operational investment, meaningfully better than the Cargo-unwatched pattern on wezterm/kanata and the github-actions-only pattern on kamal.

This is Info-positive — it doesn't reduce F0 severity (deserialization fix is maintainer-side code work, not dep updates) but it does remove one class of transitive-dep supply-chain risk. Worth naming explicitly alongside the governance-gap findings so the overall picture is honest.

5 of the 17 workflows are Gemini-AI named: Dispatch, Invoke, Review, Scheduled Triage, Triage. Google's Gemini handles automated issue triage and PR review comments. Distinctive from QuickLook's pattern (entry 22, where Copilot SWE agent authors PRs) — here the AI reviews human-authored PRs rather than producing its own contributions.

The governance interpretation: the 32.4% any-review rate likely reflects Gemini's PR comments alongside human review; the 9.5% formal APPROVED rate is human-only. This explains the gap between the two numbers — one counts AI interaction, the other counts human endorsement. Neither substitutes for enforced branch protection (F1), but the AI-triage tier is a distinct governance component worth naming.

Scanner gaps specific to this scan: Gradle parsing is not implemented, so runtime_count=0 and osv total_vulns=0 despite real dep graph evidenced by active Dependabot (F4). Distribution-channel detection surfaced only 'docker-local-build' (fictitious) when actual channels are GitHub Releases JAR + Windows MSI + Docker images on ghcr.io. Same language-ecosystem-gap pattern as Go (Xray-core), Ruby (kamal), Rust (wezterm/kanata), C# (QuickLook).

Unlike the deserialization regex (which worked correctly for Java and surfaced F0 cleanly), the dep-graph + distribution pathway for Java/Maven is not covered by the harness. Mitigation: the maintainer-side Dependabot layer (F4) largely closes this gap in practice — the CVE-watch that the harness would provide is already happening via dependabot.yml.
